1:
On Matthew 5:1 — Jesus going up the mountain, sitting, and his disciples coming to him — the witnesses divide between the fathers’ symbolic reading of the mountain and the grammatical-historical reconstruction of the scene, with the Reformed settling the harmony with Luke.

The Greek fathers read the setting morally. Chrysostom says that by choosing “not… the city, and the market place, but a mountain in a desert,” Christ teaches us “to do nothing with ostentation, and to depart from crowds” when we discourse of serious things; he also hears a fulfilment of “get thee up into a mountain” (Isaiah 40:9), that teacher and hearer alike must “stand on a high ground of spiritual virtues.” The Latin fathers add layers: Augustine reads the mountain as the “greater commandments” given by the Son to a people he would “deliver by love,” over against the lesser given through the prophets to a people “under the bondage of fear”; Hilary, that he commands “from the loftiness of his Father’s Majesty”; Rabanus, mystically, that the sitting is the Incarnation, “for had he not taken flesh… mankind could not have come unto him”; Gregory marks that “he who had before opened the mouth of the Prophets” now opens his own.

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the place and posture. Robertson insists on the article — “the mountain,” not “a mountain” — the one there by Capernaum, “the Sinai of the New Testament” (Delitzsch); and harmonizes the purposes, that Luke has him go up to pray and choose the Twelve (Luke 6:12; Mark 3:13) before teaching. Barnes locates the hill near Capernaum and notes the discourse’s parallel in Luke 6:17–20, suspecting it was repeated on different occasions. Against the tradition that placed it on Tabor (Jerome) or the Mount of Olives (which Jerome rejects), they keep it a Galilean hill. Ellicott frames the whole as the “great pattern-discourse” set at the fore-front of Matthew’s record.

The Reformed Calvin settles the chief debate: the sermon here and that in Luke 6 are not two but one, both Evangelists collecting “into one place the leading points of the doctrine of Christ” on the godly life, so the “mountain” and “plain” raise no real contradiction. Gill and Wesley note simply that the “disciples” here are not the Twelve only but “all who desired to learn of him,” and Henry hails the Preacher as “the Prince of preachers… the eternal Wisdom” (John 1:18) excelling the prophets and John.
2:
Matthew 5:2 — “he opened his mouth, and taught them” — is brief, and the comment is shared between the grammatical-historical and Reformed voices, who agree the phrase marks a solemn, deliberate discourse.

Robertson notes the inchoative imperfect “began to teach,” and that Jesus “sat down on the mountain side as the Jewish rabbis did instead of standing”: a most impressive scene as he “opened his mouth wide and spoke loud enough for the great throng to hear,” the newly chosen Twelve and “a great number of the people” before him (Luke 6:17). Gill reads in the opening of the mouth that “he spake with a clear and strong voice, that all the people might hear him… with great freedom, utterance, and cheerfulness,” on “things of the greatest moment.”

The Reformed Calvin observes the phrase is a Hebrew idiom — a redundancy (pleonasm) used “to draw attention to any thing important and remarkable” — and dismisses the allegorical reading that makes the mountain-teaching a lesson to “elevate the mind above worldly cares.” The Wesleyan Wesley draws the heart of the discourse from this solemn opening: “to bless men, to make men happy, was the great business for which our Lord came into the world,” so he “pronounces eight blessings together,” applying to the universal instinct for happiness and “directing it to its proper object,” since men miss it “because they seek it where it is not to be found.” Wesley calls the discourse that follows “the complete art of happiness,” observing that the “opening of the mouth” is a phrase that “always denotes a set and solemn discourse.” Robertson adds that the audience was no small band — “the newly chosen twelve apostles… a great number of disciples and a great number of the people” (Luke 6:17) — so that the seated, deliberate teaching of the Beatitudes was delivered with the gravity of a lawgiver to a vast and varied throng. Calvin, meanwhile, sets aside as a “frivolous” speculation the notion that the mountain-teaching was meant allegorically to lift the mind above worldly cares; the phrase is simply a solemn Hebrew opening. Gill hears in it that Christ spoke “with great freedom, utterance, and cheerfulness,” on matters “of the greatest moment and importance” — the manner befitting the weight of all that follows.
3:
On Matthew 5:3 — “Blessed are the poor in spirit” — the witnesses agree the poverty is inward, not merely material, and the chief debate is over its relation to Luke’s simpler “blessed are the poor.”

The Latin Leo states the controlling point: it “would perhaps be doubtful what poor he was speaking of” had he said only “blessed are the poor”; but “poor in spirit” shows the kingdom belongs “to those who are recommended by the humility of their souls,” not to all who suffer “hard and heavy necessity.” The grammatical-historical commentators weigh the two Gospels. Barnes notes Luke has simply “the poor,” and that the gospel is preached to the poor (Isaiah 61:1; Matthew 11:5), yet concludes the blessing is on the humble. Ellicott locates the poverty “in the region of life” named: those who, “whatever their outward state,” feel “they have nothing of their own… must be receivers before they give.” Robertson and Clarke handle the word makarios — truly “happy,” though English has “ennobled ‘blessed’ to a higher rank” (Clarke offering a fanciful etymology of immortality beyond “fate”).

The Reformed voices read it of the afflicted humble. Calvin: those are happy “who, chastened and subdued by afflictions, submit themselves wholly to God, and, with inward humility, betake themselves to him for protection,” and since Luke and Matthew “must have the same meaning,” the “poor in spirit” are the lowly emptied of “confidence in the flesh.” Gill: all are spiritually poor, but the blessed are those made “sensible of this their condition.” The Wesleyan Wesley defines them as “the unfeignedly penitent… deeply sensible of their sinfulness, guiltiness, helplessness.” The Puritan Henry frames the whole series: Christ “begins his sermon with blessings, for he came into the world to bless us” (Acts 3:26), so that the gospel opens with a blessing where the Old Testament closed with a curse (Malachi 4:6); his pronouncing them happy, Henry adds, “makes them so, for those whom he blesses are blessed indeed.” Gill enlarges the portrait of spiritual poverty: all mankind are “spiritually poor… in debt, owe ten thousand talents, and have nothing to pay,” yet most are “insensible of this their condition,” the blessed being those made to feel it. All agree the first step of the kingdom is to know one’s spiritual bankruptcy and to be thereby driven to God.
4:
On Matthew 5:4 — “Blessed are they that mourn” — the witnesses largely agree the mourning meant is godly sorrow over sin, though the grammatical-historical voices allow a wider reference, and all read the “comfort” as the work of the Spirit.

The Latin and Greek fathers are emphatic that this is sorrow for sin. Hilary: those who mourn “not loss of kindred… but who weep for past sins”; Jerome, that it is grief “for the dead in sins,” as Paul mourned the unrepentant; Ambrose places it third, after poverty and meekness, as the penitent’s mourning. Pseudo-Chrysostom adds that those who weep for others’ sins — as teachers should — are “much more” blessed. Augustine reads it as the sorrow of those “turned to God” who “lose the things that they held dear in this world,” comforted by “the Paraclete… Comforter” with eternal joys. Leo distinguishes it sharply from “the affliction of this world”: “religious grief mourns sin.”

The grammatical-historical commentators hold both senses. Barnes: it is “capable of two meanings” — the afflicted and those who mourn sin — but since Christ came “to induce men to mourn over their sins,” the latter is chiefly in view (2 Corinthians 7:10), while the gospel alone truly comforts affliction (Isaiah 61:1–3). Ellicott marks the implied limitation: “not the sorrow of the world that worketh death… but the mourning over sin itself,” comforted with “the sense of pardon and peace.” Robertson, noting the verb’s use for mourning “the sorrows and sins of others,” calls it a paradox: “there can be no comfort where there is no grief.”

The Reformed Calvin presses the paradox against worldly judgment: Christ shows that the mourner’s very sorrow “contributes to a happy life, by preparing them to receive eternal joy” and driving them to seek joy in God alone — tribulation working patience and hope (Romans 5:3–5). Gill enumerates the griefs — over indwelling sin, unbelief, daily infirmities, and the sins of the world and of professors. The Wesleyan Wesley sums it: they “shall be comforted… more solidly even in this world, and eternally in heaven.”
5:
On Matthew 5:5 — “Blessed are the meek, for they shall inherit the earth” — the witnesses agree on the nature of meekness and divide chiefly over what “the earth” means.

On meekness all converge. Augustine: the meek “resist not wrongs, and give way to evil; but overcome evil of good.” Ambrose calls it the noble government of temper, “to check anger” being the mark of a strong mind. The grammatical-historical commentators refine it: Barnes, that it is “patience in the reception of injuries… neither meanness, nor a surrender of our rights, nor cowardice,” citing Christ’s own protest (John 18:23) and Paul’s (Acts 16:37); Ellicott recalls Aristotle’s definition of resentment “under control”; Robertson, that Jesus “lifted the word to a nobility never attained before” — “the gentleness of strength, not mere effeminacy” (Matthew 11:29).

On “the earth” the fathers offer a range. Chrysostom keeps it partly literal and present, quoting the source text (Psalm 37:11): the meek man “shall possess his own in security,” while the violent often lose all. Augustine reads “the fixedness of a perpetual inheritance” in which the soul rests. Pseudo-Chrysostom canvasses the options — the earth freed from corruption, or heaven, or the glorified body. Hilary and Leo take “the earth” as “the flesh of the saints,” changed in “a happy resurrection” and “clothed with the glory of immortality.” Ellicott hears an echo of the kingdom given to the saints in Daniel 7:27.

The Reformed Calvin marks the paradox: by worldly reckoning “those who warmly repel any attacks… claim for themselves the dominion of the earth,” yet Christ promises it to the gentle, against “the diabolical proverb” that we must “howl with the wolves.” Gill commends meekness even from Jewish witness, defining the meek as those “not easily provoked to anger… who have the meanest thoughts of themselves, and the best of others.” Robertson notes the ancients “did not rank it as a virtue” at all, prizing rather a hard self-assertion, so that here too “the Beatitudes assume a new heart.” Wesley reads the promise that they shall “enjoy whatever portion God hath given them here, and shall hereafter possess the new earth, wherein dwelleth righteousness.”
6:
On Matthew 5:6 — “Blessed are they which do hunger and thirst after righteousness” — the witnesses agree the figure expresses intense desire, and divide gently on what righteousness is meant.

The Latin and Greek fathers stress the earnestness. Ambrose places it after mourning — “as soon as I have wept for my sins, I begin to hunger and thirst” — and Jerome insists desire is not enough “unless we also suffer hunger for it,” since “we are never righteous enough.” Pseudo-Chrysostom distinguishes hungering “to walk according to the righteousness of God” from thirsting “to get the knowledge thereof.” Chrysostom allows either “general righteousness” or the particular virtue opposed to covetousness, ascribing to righteousness “that which is peculiar to avarice” — hunger and thirst. Augustine reads the food as Christ’s own, “to do the will of my Father” (John 4:34), and the drink as the well “springing up to life eternal” (John 4:14); Leo, that the soul “desires to be satiated with… righteousness… and be filled with the Lord himself” (Psalm 34:8).

The grammatical-historical commentators draw out the image. Barnes notes no wants “so imperiously demand supply” as hunger and thirst, fit emblems of the deep sense of sin and want (Psalm 42:1–2). Ellicott hears the lesson of the wilderness, the craving become “a parable of that higher yearning after righteousness… certain in the end to gain its full fruition” (John 4:14). Robertson glosses “filled” (chortasthēsontai), to feed or fatten, turning “one of the elemental human instincts to spiritual use.”

The Reformed Calvin reads “hunger and thirst” as a figure of the part for the whole — to “suffer poverty… and even to be defrauded of one’s right” — so that Matthew makes “one class stand for all the rest,” the blessed being those who, though they desire “nothing but what is proper,” languish as if famishing. Jerome warns that we are “never righteous enough, but always hunger after works of righteousness,” and Chrysostom adds that he who so loves righteousness “possesses all things in safety,” covetousness only seeming to enrich. Wesley reads it plainly of hungering “after the holiness here described,” the promise being that “they shall be satisfied with it.”
7:
On Matthew 5:7 — “Blessed are the merciful, for they shall obtain mercy” — the witnesses agree on the reciprocity of the promise and on a broad definition of mercy.

The Latin fathers place it in the order of virtues. The Gloss: “justice and mercy are so united… justice without mercy is cruelty; mercy without justice, profusion.” Remigius defines the merciful as one who “counts others’ misery his own”; Jerome widens mercy beyond alms to “every sin of a brother, if we bear one another’s burdens”; Hilary, that God “will bestow his own mercy only on the merciful.” Leo presses the wonder of the exchange — “mercy wishes thee to be merciful… that the image of God may be reflected in the mirror of the human heart.” The Greek Chrysostom notes the reward only “seems at first to be an equal return,” but is far more, “for human mercy and divine mercy are not to be put on an equality.”

The grammatical-historical commentators expand the definition. Clarke traces it through the Latin misericordia — “pain of heart” at another’s misery — covering both “the pardon of injuries, and almsgiving.” Barnes makes it “an evidence of piety,” done “to glorify God” (Matthew 10:42; Matthew 25:34–40). Ellicott links it to the Lord’s Prayer and to James 2:13 — “no motive to mercy is so constraining as the feeling that we ourselves needed it and have found it.”

The Reformed Calvin again marks the paradox against worldly wisdom, which reckons happy “those who give themselves no concern about the distresses of others”; Christ counts blessed those who “take a share in the afflictions of others… clothe themselves, as it were, with the same affections.” Gill itemizes mercy to the bodies of men — sympathizing and distributing “with readiness and cheerfulness” — and to their souls, by instructing, reproving, forgiving, and comforting. Robertson renders the promise in dialect, “sal win pitie theirsels,” calling it “a self-acting law of the moral world” (Bruce). Wesley sums it: “whatever mercy we desire from God, the same let us show to our brethren… he will repay us a thousand fold.”
8:
On Matthew 5:8 — “Blessed are the pure in heart, for they shall see God” — the witnesses agree the purity is inward, against Pharisaic ceremonial cleanness, and that the promised vision of God is the crown of the Beatitudes.

On the purity, all locate it in the heart. The Gloss sets it “in the sixth place, because on the sixth day man was created in the image of God,” reformed anew “in pure hearts by grace.” Chrysostom takes the pure as those “conscious to themselves of no evil thoughts,” living in the holiness “without which no man shall see God” (Hebrews 12:14). The grammatical-historical commentators sharpen the contrast with the Pharisees: Gill, that men “may have pure notions and impure hearts”; Lightfoot, mocking the Pharisee “whose praise lies in outward cleanness”; Clarke, that they “affected outward purity, while their hearts were full of corruption.” Ellicott and Robertson take purity in “its widest sense” — not only the absence of sensual sin but the exclusion of “the impurity of hate or greed of gain.”

On the vision of God the Latin Augustine is fullest: God “is seen only by the heart,” not the bodily eye; the “single heart” is the pure heart, made clean “by faith” (Acts 15:9), and the seeing is “the reward of faith.” Pseudo-Chrysostom: the righteous “see God” now in part, “but in that world to come… face to face, not in a glass” (1 Corinthians 13:12). Barnes notes that to “see God’s face” was, among the Jews, a term of high favour (Revelation 22:4); Robertson, that “the Beatific Vision” belongs to the pure in heart, “sin befogs the heart so that one cannot see God.”

The Reformed Calvin reads purity as sincerity against the world’s prized craftiness: the blessed are those who “converse sincerely with men, and express nothing… which they do not feel in their heart.” Wesley defines them as “the sanctified… who love God with all their hearts,” who “shall see God… in all things here, hereafter in glory.” Chrysostom guards that “mercy alone is not enough,” adding purity because “there are many merciful, yet unchaste”; and the Gloss sets this beatitude’s reward above the former — “not merely to dine in the King’s court, but further to see his face.” The vision promised, all agree, is reserved in its fullness for the world to come, yet begun now in the believing, cleansed heart.
9:
On Matthew 5:9 — “Blessed are the peacemakers, for they shall be called the sons of God” — the witnesses agree on a peace that begins within and works outward, and on the adoption it earns.

The Latin and Greek fathers root peace within. Jerome and Ambrose insist one must “make peace first within their own hearts, then between brethren at variance,” for “what avails it to make peace between others, while in your own heart are wars of rebellious vices?” Augustine defines peace as “the fixedness of order,” the soul whose reason has subdued its “carnal desires.” Pseudo-Chrysostom widens it to those who, “unmindful of wrongs, cultivate peace.” On the reward, Hilary calls it “the reward of adoption,” since we enter God’s family only “by living in brotherly love”; Chrysostom grounds it in Christ’s own work — “to reconcile things separated” was “the chief employment of the Only-begotten Son,” so peacemakers are rightly his brethren. The Gloss sets it seventh, answering to the sabbath rest after the six ages.

The grammatical-historical commentators stress the active grace. Ellicott distinguishes the merely “peaceable” temper (James 3:17) from this “higher form… acting energetically upon others” (Acts 7:26; Romans 12:18), and notes it rightly follows purity of heart, “for it is the absence of all baseness… that gives the power to make peace.” Robertson: not merely “peaceable men” but those who “bring peace where it is not” — “the perfect peacemaker is the Son of God” (Ephesians 2:14). Clarke traces the very word “peace” to “connecting into one.”

The Reformed Calvin notes the thankless cost: reconcilers “endure the indignity of hearing reproaches… on all sides,” so Christ bids them “look up to the judgment of God.” Wesley: they “do all possible good to all men,” and shall be “acknowledged” God’s children — though he warns this amiable temper will not be the world’s darling while Satan reigns. Leo qualifies it: the peace meant is only that which agrees with God’s will (Psalm 119:165), not “leagues in crimes.” The Alexandrian Origen, as an outlier, reads the peacemaker also as one who, finding “nothing crooked” in the divine oracles, reconciles the seeming conflicts of Scripture — Law with Prophets, Old with New.
10:
On Matthew 5:10 — “Blessed are they which are persecuted for righteousness’ sake” — the witnesses agree the qualifier “for righteousness” is decisive, and several note how this eighth beatitude closes the series by returning to the first.

The Greek Chrysostom takes “righteousness” broadly — “for virtue, for defending others, for piety” — and sets it after the peacemakers “that we may not suppose it is good to seek peace at all times.” He notes the kingdom is named here as in the first beatitude though “tacitly understood” throughout. The Latin Augustine makes the structure explicit: the eighth beatitude “returns to the commencement, because it shows the perfect complete character” — the seven steps making the perfect man, the eighth proving him — and aligns them with the sevenfold Spirit of Isaiah 11:2. Jerome stresses that “for righteousness’ sake” is added “expressly, for many suffer persecution for their sins, and are not therefore righteous.” Pseudo-Chrysostom widens the sufferers beyond those persecuted by Gentiles to any who suffer from heretics, or even from corrupt “great ones” among Christians, for refusing to forsake the truth.

The grammatical-historical commentators guard the same point. Barnes: “we are not to seek persecution” nor provoke it by “strange sentiments,” but if it comes for an honest Christian life it is blessed. Robertson bluntly: “posing as persecuted is a favourite stunt”; the kingdom belongs only to those who suffer “for the sake of goodness, not who are guilty of wrong.” Ellicott notes the suggestiveness that “the last promise is the same as the first” — we end as we began with the kingdom of heaven, having learned that it “includes all” the intervening blessings.

The Reformed Calvin presses the hard necessity: “we cannot be Christ’s soldiers on any other condition than to have the greater part of the world rising in hostility against us,” for Satan “will never cease to fill his followers with rage.” Wesley, citing 2 Timothy 3:12, agrees that all who “live godly in Christ Jesus shall suffer persecution,” the world ever saying, “Away with such fellows from the earth.” Pseudo-Chrysostom widens the blessing further: not only those killed by Gentiles but whoever suffers from heretics, or even from corrupt “great ones” among Christians, for refusing to forsake the truth, “has the reward of martyrdom though he suffers from his own people.” Ambrose reads the doubled naming of the kingdom (first and eighth) as the whole course of blessedness, beginning here and “perfected” in the resurrection life with Christ.
11:
On Matthew 5:11 — “Blessed are ye, when men shall revile you… falsely, for my sake” — the witnesses note the shift to direct address and the two conditions that make a true martyr’s blessing.

Rabanus and Ellicott mark the turn: “here, for the first time, the beatitude is uttered, not as a general law, but as the portion of the listening disciples.” Augustine distinguishes the three forms of suffering — reviling (insult “in the face of one present”), persecution (“open violence and secret snares”), and slander against the absent — and warns against those who “boast of persecutions” though the evil said of them is either true or not for Christ’s sake. Ellicott lists the same grades, down to “deliberate calumnies, such as those of the foul orgies… spread against the believers in the first two centuries.”

The two conditions are pressed by all. Pseudo-Chrysostom: the blessing requires that the evil spoken be “false” and “for God’s sake.” Robertson notes the same — “both things must be true before one can wear a martyr’s crown” — while Ellicott observes that “falsely” is absent from the best manuscripts, probably added as a safeguard. Chrysostom hears in “for my sake” Christ’s “equality in honour with his Father” (“as they suffered for my Father, so shall ye suffer for me”), and in “the prophets before you” that the apostles are themselves now become prophets. Gregory counsels enduring slander “with equanimity” while sometimes checking defamers “lest they corrupt the innocent.”

The grammatical-historical Barnes lays the emphasis on “falsely”: it is “not blessed to have evil spoken of us if we deserve it,” recalling that Christ, reviled, “reviled not again” (1 Peter 2:23; 1 Corinthians 4:12). The Reformed Calvin offers comfort against being “thrown out of the Church as ungodly”: believers need not dread even the Pope’s excommunication, since “there is no class of men more envenomed than hypocrites,” and Christ foresaw “with what furious madness the enemies of the Gospel would attack his small and despised flock.” Gill reads the verse as prophetic of what “the first Christians particularly” would endure, designed to “animate and fortify them.” Barnes lays the whole weight on “falsely”: it is no blessing “to have evil spoken of us if we deserve it,” but to be reproached untruly for Christ is to “tread in the footsteps of Jesus” (Philippians 1:29).
12:
On Matthew 5:12 — “Rejoice, and be exceeding glad, for great is your reward in heaven” — the witnesses agree the command is to exultant joy, grounded in the heavenly reward and the company of the prophets, and the Reformed guard the reward as one of grace.

The grammatical-historical commentators draw out the intensity. Clarke renders “be exceeding glad” as “leap for joy,” recalling “several cases on record where this was literally done by the martyrs in Queen Mary’s days,” and cites the rabbinic saying of Rabbi Tarphon that “the reward is great.” Ellicott notes the second word implies “a glorious and exulting joy,” echoed in 1 Peter 1:8 and 4:13. Barnes observes that to those who suffer most “God imparts the highest rewards,” so that the early Christians sometimes “rejoiced, and leaped for joy, at the prospect of death for the sake of Jesus” — evidence of “something in religion to sustain the soul which the world does not possess.”

On the example, the Gloss and Remigius note the comfort of the prophets’ precedent — “remember that ye are his apostles, of whom also they were prophets” — the recollection of others’ sufferings strengthening the sufferer.

The Reformed Calvin and Ellicott guard against a doctrine of merit. Calvin: “there is not (as the Papists dream) a mutual relation between the reward and merit”; given the “imperfections and faults” of even the best works, “there will be no work which God can judge to be worthy of reward,” the promise being “free.” Ellicott concurs that even here the reward is “not of debt, but of grace” (Luke 17:10; Romans 4:4), promised to those who suffer “for righteousness, for Christ,” not to those “calculating on a future compensation.” The remedy against unjust reproach, Calvin adds, is to “raise our minds to heaven,” where “vast grounds of joy” dispel sadness. Gill grounds the gladness in the honour put upon the sufferers and “the glory and happiness that shall follow”; Barnes observes that to the greatest sufferers “God imparts the highest rewards,” so the martyrs counted their crown the brightest. Wesley marks the reward as “over and above the happiness that naturally results from holiness” — joy doubled, in the thing itself and in its heavenly recompense.
13:
On Matthew 5:13 — “Ye are the salt of the earth” — the witnesses agree the figure marks the apostles’ preserving office, and read the lost savour as the apostate teacher’s ruin.

The Greek Chrysostom hears in it both comfort and charge: after such sublime precepts, lest they despair, Christ shows the precepts “necessary… not for your own salvation merely… but for the whole world”; and as salt is “rough and biting,” so they must reprove, not “flatter.” The Latin fathers develop the image: Hilary calls them “salters with eternity,” preserving bodies for it by their teaching; Jerome presses the warning — “if the doctor have erred, by what other doctor shall he be corrected?” Remigius allegorizes salt’s making by water, sun, and wind into baptism, love, and the Spirit.

The grammatical-historical commentators ground the figure in fact. Barnes: salt “preserves from putrefaction,” so Christians keep “the world from entire moral corruption.” Robertson glosses “lost its savour” (mōranthē, literally “to play the fool,” of salt become insipid), noting salt scattered useless in piles — perhaps “a current proverb” (Mark 9:50). Clarke and Ellicott explain from travellers that the salt of Jebel-Usdum by the Dead Sea really does lose its savour where rain reaches it. Wesley insists it is “not the apostles, not ministers only, but all ye who are thus holy.” Henry notes the apostles, unlike the prophets (“the salt of the land of Canaan”), are “the salt of the whole earth.”

The Latin Augustine and the Reformed Calvin read the savourless salt as the teacher who falls. Augustine: not he that suffers persecution “is trodden under foot… but he who through fear of persecution falls away.” Calvin: doctrine is “so closely connected with a good conscience… that the corruption which might be tolerated in others would in them be detestable and monstrous” — fit only “to be cast out from the office of teacher” (the Gloss). Chrysostom presses the application: when reviled for reproving men, the apostles must “rejoice; for this is the proper effect of salt,” which bites. Henry encourages the persecuted with this very dignity — though “treated with contempt,” they are “really blessings to the world,” the apostles being “the salt of the whole earth” sent “into all the world to preach the gospel.”
14:
On Matthew 5:14 — “Ye are the light of the world… a city that is set on an hill” — the witnesses agree the disciples shine with a derived light and are set in the world’s view to enlighten it.

The Latin and Greek fathers distinguish the two figures. Pseudo-Chrysostom: “to live well must go before to teach well,” so the apostles, first “salt” to preserve, are now “light” to bring men “into a better state by enlightening” — salt toward the Jews who knew God, light toward the Gentiles. Hilary and Bede read “the city” as the flesh Christ took, or the Church set on the height; Chrysostom marvels at the promise that men “scarce known in their own country” should have their fame reach “the ends of the earth,” persecution making the light “but more conspicuous.” Jerome draws the charge of boldness — not “hidden through fear, like lamps under a corn-measure,” but declaring on the housetops what was heard in secret.

The grammatical-historical commentators add the local color and the Christological qualification. Barnes: as the sun makes objects visible, so Christians “show what God requires… the way that leads to heaven.” Clarke notes “light of the world” was a title of eminent rabbis, which Christ transfers to his disciples, and points (with Maundrell) to the “Mount of Beatitudes” near Tabor. Ellicott reads the word in its truest sense as belonging to Christ (John 8:12), the disciples shining “with a derived brightness,” and suggests the Lord may have pointed to Safed on its hill.

The Reformed Calvin agrees the designation belongs peculiarly to the apostles and pastors, though all believers are “children of light” (Luke 12:35); they are placed on high “on the condition that they shall shine… on all others.” Wesley: if holy, “you can no more be hid than the sun in the firmament,” nor than “a city on a mountain.” Bede deepens the figure: Christ himself “lighted this lamp, when he filled the earthen vessel of human nature with the fire of his Divinity,” the lampstand being the Church on which the lamp is set. Gill notes the Jews gave this very title — “light of the world” — to their sanhedrin and learned doctors, which Christ “more truly applies” to his apostles, who carried the Gospel’s light across a world “in great darkness of ignorance and error.”
15:
On Matthew 5:15 — the lamp not put under the bushel but on the stand — the comment is brief and chiefly grammatical-historical, fixing the homely image and its purpose.

Robertson corrects the rendering: it is “the bushel” and “the lamp-stand,” not “a candle… candlestick.” “The figure is taken from lowly cottage life” — a single room with one earthenware grain-measure and one lamp set “on a projecting stone in the wall,” so that to put it under the bushel was “to put it out or to hide it.” Ellicott notes the word is a portable lamp rather than a wax candle, the cheaper vessel of the poor (Wiclif’s “lantern”), and that the definite articles imply “objects familiar to all the hearers.” Clarke adds from ancient writers that “only those who had bad designs hid a candle under a bushel,” to keep light hidden for night-time mischief.

The Reformed Gill reads the lamp as “the Gospel, and gifts qualifying men to preach it,” lit in the evening of the Jewish dispensation and not to be confined or concealed but to give light throughout the world. Wesley draws the conclusion plainly: “the very design of God in giving you this light was, that it might shine” (Mark 4:21; Luke 8:16). Barnes makes the same point: men light a lamp “not to conceal the light, but… that it may be of use,” and so religion “is given that we may benefit others… not to be concealed, but suffered to shed light on a surrounding wicked world.” Clarke adds from antiquity that “only those who had bad designs hid a candle under a bushel,” reserving its light for deeds of darkness — the very opposite of the disciple’s calling. Ellicott notes the “candle” was really a portable oil-lamp, “the cheaper vessel of the poor” (Wiclif’s “lantern”), and that the definite articles — “the bushel,” “the lamp-stand” — point to the single furnishings of a humble one-room house, familiar to every hearer. The verse thus turns the privilege of light into the duty of shining: a lamp lit only to be hidden is a contradiction in terms.
16:
On Matthew 5:16 — “Let your light so shine before men… that they may… glorify your Father” — the witnesses agree that the aim of shining is not self-display but the glory of God, and several reconcile it with the later warning against ostentation.

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the syntax and motive. Ellicott corrects the English: it is “Thus, like the lamp on its stand, let your light shine,” the motive being “the direct opposite” of the Pharisee’s — not “to be seen of men” but to win them to “glorify the Giver of the light.” Robertson: “light shines to see others by, not to call attention to itself.” Barnes presses that we are to do good works not “merely to be seen” (which is “pride and ostentation,” Matthew 6:1) but that “God may be honoured”; true Christians “act to glorify God, and care little what men may think” of them save as it serves that end. Clarke adds that “it is not sufficient to have light — we must walk in the light, and by the light,” the whole conduct being “a perpetual comment” on the doctrine professed. Gill notes the light given the disciples was “not for their own private use, but for the public good of mankind.”

The Reformed Calvin reconciles this command with the charge to do good works in secret (Matthew 6:4): the latter “is intended only to forbid ostentation,” while here the object is “to recommend the glory of God alone” — believers must “provide for honest things… in the sight of men” (2 Corinthians 8:21). The two precepts, rightly read, do not collide: the one forbids working in order to be seen, the other bids us live so that what is seen redounds to God’s honour, not our own. Wesley: that seeing the good works, men “may be moved to love and serve God likewise.”

The Alexandrian Origen, as an outlier, expands “shine” eschatologically: the righteous shall shine, yet “differ in glory” as the stars (Daniel 12:3; 1 Corinthians 15:41). A stray Cyril of Jerusalem fragment attached here concerns the angels’ vision of God and is unrelated to the verse.
17:
On Matthew 5:17 — “Think not that I am come to destroy the law or the prophets… but to fulfil” — the witnesses agree Christ confirms rather than abolishes the Law, and divide gently on the senses of “fulfil.”

The grammatical-historical commentators explain why the disclaimer was needed. John Chrysostom asks “who suspected this?” and answers that, since Christ was about to ordain commandments “greater than those of old” (“but I say unto you”), he forestalls the charge lest “the strangeness” alarm. Lightfoot grounds it in Jewish expectation: the nation looked for a Messiah who would “advance the Mosaic law to the very highest pitch,” so the scribes cavilled that Jesus, in abolishing their traditions, was no true Messiah. Robertson glosses the verbs: “destroy” is to “loosen down” a house (2 Corinthians 5:1), “fulfil” is to “fill full” — “to reveal the full depth of meaning.” Clarke takes “fulfil” as to “complete… accomplish every thing shadowed forth in the Mosaic ritual” and fill up the moral law’s design.

The Greek and Latin fathers name the ways of fulfilling. Chrysostom: Christ fulfilled the prophets “by accomplishing what was foretold concerning himself,” and the Law “by transgressing none of its precepts” and by “justifying by faith, which the Law could not do by the letter.” Augustine finds a double sense — to fulfil “by adding something which it had not, or by doing what it commands” — and, since “Thou shalt not lust” was beyond fallen strength, Christ “obtained for us this indulgence” that his perfection should supply our infirmity. Jerome and Gregory turn it against the Pharisees who “say, and do not.”

The Reformed Calvin clarifies that the passage “treats of doctrine, not of life”: Christ’s teaching “is so far from being at variance with the Law… that it agrees perfectly” and brings its “complete” meaning, against those who slandered the gospel as the Law’s ruin. Gill notes the address has now widened to “the whole multitude,” many fearing the new light meant to set Moses aside; and Henry frames the rule Christ establishes as exactly agreeing with “the law and the prophets,” which he “keeps on the throne.” Wesley confines it to “the moral law,” which Christ came “to establish, illustrate, and explain… both by my life and doctrine.”
18:
On Matthew 5:18 — “till heaven and earth pass, one jot or one tittle shall in no wise pass from the law” — the witnesses agree on the Law’s indestructible permanence and dwell on the smallness of jot and tittle.

The grammatical-historical commentators handle the terms. Barnes: the “jot” is yod, “the smallest letter in the Hebrew alphabet,” and the “tittle” a small point distinguishing one letter from another, so the saying means “everything else may change — the very earth and heaven may pass away — but the law of God shall not be destroyed, till its whole design shall be accomplished.” Robertson notes the iota is the smallest Greek vowel standing for the Hebrew yod, and the “tittle” a tiny stroke or hook, citing the rabbinic dread that to alter one “the world would be destroyed.” Clarke draws the contrast of the temporal and eternal — “heaven… and earth shall pass away… but the word of the Lord endureth for ever” (2 Corinthians 4:18).

On “verily” (Amen), the commentators dwell on the word’s first appearance. Ellicott traces it to the Hebrew root for “fixed, steadfast, true,” used in solemn affirmation in the wilderness and Temple (Numbers 5:22; Psalm 41:13) and passing thence into the Church’s worship. Gill and Lightfoot note that Christ uses it “by the highest divine right,” being himself “Amen, the faithful witness” (Revelation 3:14; 2 Corinthians 1:20).

On the limit “till all be fulfilled,” the Reformed Calvin reports but declines the “ingenious refinements” that make the last judgment end the Law; he reads it simply, that “the truth of the law and of every part of it is secure,” nothing in the world being “so durable.” Wesley adds that the Law “has its effect” both when its precepts are obeyed and when its rewards and punishments are executed (Luke 16:17; Luke 21:33). Hilary infers from “pass” that the elements of heaven and earth shall not be annihilated but renewed, and Remigius that the letters “shall abide in their essence, but pass through renewal.” The cumulative force, all agree, is the unshakeable stability of God’s word: heaven and earth are the most enduring things the eye knows, yet the smallest stroke of the Law outlasts them.
19:
On Matthew 5:19 — whoever breaks and teaches others to break “one of these least commandments” shall be called least — the witnesses agree the warning falls on teachers, and that the “least commandments” are still really great.

The grammatical-historical commentators set the rule against Pharisaic casuistry. Barnes and Clarke note the Pharisees “divided the precepts of the law into lesser and greater” (Matthew 23:23), teaching that violating the former was “a trivial offence”; Christ counters that he who so teaches “shall be least.” Ellicott resolves an apparent difficulty: though the words might seem to bind even the ceremonial law, the usage of the time confined “commandment” to the moral law (so throughout the New Testament; compare Romans 13:9), and the context deals only with morals — the “least” being those “which seemed trivial, yet were really great,” the control of thoughts and desires. Robertson presses the order “do and teach”: “Jesus puts practice before preaching,” against the scribes who “say, and do not” (Matthew 23:3).

The Latin fathers read it of the teacher’s example. Gregory: in the Church “that teacher who breaks a commandment is called least, because he whose life is despised… his preaching be also despised.” Jerome: “the learning of the master, if joined with sin however small, loses him the highest place” — “perfect bliss is for him who fulfils in deed what he teaches in word.” Rabanus finds even the Greek iota apt, standing for ten and so alluding to the Decalogue “of which the Gospel is the point and perfection.”

The Reformed Calvin confines the verse to “the commandments of life, or the ten words”: those are “false and deceitful teachers” who do not hold their disciples to the Law and who “weaken, in the slightest degree, the authority of the law,” while the “honest and faithful” recommend its keeping “both by word and by example.” Wesley: he who breaks the least and so teaches “shall have no part” in the kingdom. Pseudo-Chrysostom resolves why the same man is called “least” rather than excluded outright: to break the least and not keep it are one, yet the lawbreaker who still owns the Law’s authority differs from him who, like the scribes, abolishes it. Gill confines “these least commandments” to “the precepts of the law, of which some were comparatively lesser than others,” broken when “men acted contrary to them” — so the warning falls hardest on the teacher whose practice belies his preaching.
20:
On Matthew 5:20 — “except your righteousness shall exceed the righteousness of the scribes and Pharisees… ye shall in no case enter the kingdom of heaven” — the witnesses agree the demand is for a righteousness of the heart surpassing mere external observance.

The grammatical-historical commentators define the contrast. Barnes: the scribes’ righteousness “was external… consisted in outward observances” — sacrifices, fasts, ablutions — whereas “the righteousness of true Christians is seated in the heart.” Robertson renders “exceed” as “overflow like a river out of its banks,” a “daring statement” that disciples must out-do the very professionals of piety — the scribes in teaching and the Pharisees, “the separated ones,” in practice. Ellicott notes this is the first mention of the scribes in the discourse, the “men who write” become the recognized interpreters of the Law. Clarke takes “exceed” to mean a righteousness that grasps “not only the letter, but the spirit and design” of the precept.

The Greek Chrysostom takes “righteousness” as “universal virtue,” and marvels at the “superior power of grace” requiring untaught disciples “to be better than… masters” — yet, by comparing the old with the new, he “confirms the Old Testament.” The Latin Augustine reads the “kingdom of heaven” here as the present Church, which contains both the lesser and the great, against the kingdom “where none enters but he who does” what he teaches; he glosses “exceed” as to do what the scribes merely “say, and do not” (Matthew 23:3).

The Reformed Calvin exposes the scribes’ pretended zeal for the Law — “how coldly they observe the law… while they boast… an assumed and hypocritical righteousness.” Gill notes the people held the Pharisees in high esteem “for their seeming righteousness,” which Christ pronounces insufficient. The bar set is not less law but deeper: a righteousness reaching the thoughts and the heart. Pseudo-Chrysostom draws the contrast of effect — the commandments of Moses “save from the punishment due to transgressors… but do not bring into the kingdom,” whereas Christ’s both “deliver from punishment, and bring into the kingdom.” Clarke renders “exceed” as a righteousness taking in “not only the letter, but the spirit and design” of the precept, pointing through it to Christ the “great Atonement.” Hilary warns there is “no admission to the kingdom of heaven without a righteousness beyond that of Pharisees.”
21:
On Matthew 5:21 — “Ye have heard that it was said… Thou shalt not kill… in danger of the judgment” — the witnesses agree Christ engages not Moses’s law itself but the scribes’ cramped gloss upon it.

The grammatical-historical commentators establish the formula. Gill and Lightfoot show that “ye have heard” means “ye have received it by tradition” — the rabbinic idiom for what is “learned by hearing” in the schools — so the citation is “not produced as the bare words of Moses, but… clothed in the glosses of the scribes.” Barnes agrees Jesus sets himself “not against the law of Moses, but against the false and pernicious interpretations.” On “them of old time,” Ellicott argues the better rendering is “to them of old” (the construction of Romans 9:12), pointing to the age “when synagogues began to be established.” Wesley and Clarke explain “the judgment” as the local court of twenty-three that could sentence to strangling, distinct from the Sanhedrin’s power to stone.

The Latin and Puritan voices read Christ’s method. Henry: having kept Moses on the throne and deposed the scribes, Christ now “expounds the law in particular instances… and vindicates it from the corrupt glosses,” adding nothing new but showing “the breadth, strictness, and spiritual nature” of the sixth commandment.

The Reformed Calvin makes the governing point for the whole section: this is not, as some suppose, “a correction or enlargement of the Law,” as if Christ raised his disciples above what Moses gave a “gross and carnal nation”; rather he shows “by what tortuous methods the Pharisees had debased the law by their crooked expositions,” restoring its true and spiritual sense. The contrast that follows, then, is between the scribes’ tradition and the Law rightly understood — not between Moses and Christ. Barnes notes that to the bare prohibition the scribes had attached only “the judgment,” the lower court, as if nothing but the outward act of killing were in view. Gill and Lightfoot trace the very idiom “ye have heard” to the schoolroom, where the recited text came “clothed in the glosses of the scribes” and was received “by tradition.” Christ’s quarrel, the witnesses agree, is with that received gloss, which had shrunk the sixth commandment to the magistrate’s business and left the angry heart untouched.
22:
On Matthew 5:22 — “But I say unto you, whosoever is angry with his brother… Raca… Thou fool… hell fire” — the witnesses note Christ’s tone of authority, debate the words “without a cause,” and trace the rising scale of guilt.

On the authority, the grammatical-historical commentators are struck. Robertson: “Jesus thus assumes a tone of superiority over the Mosaic regulations”; Ellicott notes the emphatic “I,” unlike the scribe who cited “this Rabbi and that” (Matthew 7:28–29); Barnes infers that one who can thus expound and change the law “must be himself Divine” (John 1:1). The chief textual debate concerns “without a cause”: Jerome, Clarke, Robertson, and Wesley hold it absent from the best manuscripts and foreign to the discourse — “anger altogether forbidden,” since even the scribes condemned causeless anger, so its retention “does not exceed theirs”; Ellicott allows it may be original, “struck out as giving too wide a margin to… excuses.”

On the rising terms, Robertson and Calvin trace three degrees. “Raca” (Aramaic, “empty”) and “thou fool” (Greek mōre) Bruce distinguishes — “Raca expresses contempt for a man’s head… fool expresses contempt for his heart and character” — answering to the judgment, the council, and the Gehenna of fire. Rabanus explains Gehenna from the defiled valley near Jerusalem; Jerome glosses Raca as “empty, vain.” Calvin reads the three not as graded penalties so much as a teaching that God “will not allow even the slightest movements of anger” to go unpunished.

The Reformed and grammatical-historical voices guard against forbidding all anger: Barnes notes anger at injury is “a natural feeling” given for self-defence, the sin being its excess and rashness. Christ’s point, all agree, is that the sixth commandment reaches the angry heart, not the murderous hand alone. Wesley insists the spurious “without a cause” must fall, for “if he had only forbidden the being angry without a cause, there was no need of that solemn declaration, I say unto you” — the scribes themselves allowed that much, so “this righteousness does not exceed theirs.” Lightfoot adds the rabbinic distinction between a “brother” (an Israelite) and a “neighbour,” which the scribes exploited to narrow the precept’s reach — another of the “crooked expositions” Christ here overturns.
23:
On Matthew 5:23 — “if… thou rememberest that thy brother hath ought against thee” — the witnesses agree reconciliation must precede worship, and note the searching direction of the command.

The Latin fathers press its inwardness. Augustine: if anger and “Raca” are forbidden, “much less is it lawful to keep in the memory any thing which might convert anger into hate.” Jerome marks the precise wording — “not, if thou hast ought against thy brother, but, if thy brother hath ought against thee” — that “the necessity of reconciliation may be more imperative.” Pseudo-Chrysostom draws out the mercy of God, who “loves concord in the faithful more than offering at his altar,” so that while dissensions last “their gift is not looked upon, their prayer is not heard.” Gregory: “he is not willing to accept sacrifice at the hands of those who are at variance.” Hilary reads the order — restore peace with men, then “return to peace with God.”

The grammatical-historical commentators set the scene. Ellicott: our Lord paints “a scene in the Jewish Temple,” the worshipper at the altar to ask “not whether he has a ground of complaint against any one, but whether any one has cause of complaint against him.” Barnes contrasts the Pharisees, “intent only on the external act,” with Christ’s teaching that “it was of more consequence to have the heart right.” Lightfoot adduces the rabbinic rule that one who has wronged a neighbour must make restitution before his offering is accepted, which Christ “extends to all offences.”

The Reformed Calvin sums it: the precept against murder is kept “when we maintain agreement and brotherly kindness”; Christ declares “even the duties of religion are displeasing to God… if we are at variance,” for “so long as a difference… is kept up by our fault, we have no access to God.” Wesley: “neither thy gift nor thy prayer will atone for thy want of love.” The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem applies the verse to the liturgical kiss of peace, “the sign that our souls are mingled together,” banishing “all remembrance of wrongs.” Chrysostom adds that if love alone will not move us to reconciliation, the desire “that our work should not remain imperfect” in the holy place should; and Pseudo-Chrysostom marks the divine condescension — God “thinks rather of man’s benefit than of his own honour,” loving “concord in the faithful more than offering at his altar.” (The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note attached here strays to the count of the Beatitudes.)
24:
On Matthew 5:24 — “leave there thy gift before the altar, and go thy way; first be reconciled to thy brother” — the witnesses agree this startling priority places reconciliation even above the unfinished act of sacrifice.

The grammatical-historical commentators feel the force of the interruption. Ellicott: to “leave the gift and the priest, the act of sacrifice unfinished, would be strange and startling, yet that… were better than to sacrifice with the sense of a wrong unconfessed,” and may allude to the Talmudic rule (Pesachim) that one who remembers leaven left in the house must return and remove it before finishing the Passover. Robertson dwells on the verb “be reconciled” (diallagēthi, second aorist passive imperative, ingressive — “take the initiative”), the only New Testament use of this compound, which “denotes mutual concession after mutual hostility”; he cites a second-century papyrus where a prodigal son uses the identical form, “I beseech thee, mother, be reconciled with me.” Lightfoot notes how unusual such leaving of a gift at the altar would be.

The Latin and Wesleyan voices draw the application. Clarke: “God will not accept of any act of religious worship… while any enmity subsists in our hearts towards any soul of man”; a religion “the very essence of which is love” cannot suffer at its altars “a heart that is revengeful.” Wesley: “neither thy gift nor thy prayer will atone for thy want of love: but this will make them both an abomination before God.” Gill notes the leaving could easily be done at the great feasts, when all Israel was gathered. The Eastern Cyril again applies it to the kiss of peace as the act of “reconciliation” before the offering. Across the voices the lesson is one: charity restored is the condition of acceptable worship. Gill notes the leaving of the gift “might easily be done” at the great feasts, “when all the Israelites were together,” so the command was no impractical counsel but a real and immediate duty. Lightfoot, with his rabbinic learning, observes how “altogether unusual” such an interruption of the sacrifice would be in Jewish practice, which only sharpens Christ’s point: reconciliation cannot wait even for the completion of an offering already begun.
25:
On Matthew 5:25 — “Agree with thine adversary quickly, whiles thou art in the way with him” — the witnesses divide between a plain reading of legal prudence and the fathers’ allegorical applications, both serving the lesson of reconciliation.

The grammatical-historical commentators take it literally. Gill warns against reading it allegorically (adversary = justice, judge = God, prison = hell), insisting “the design is to prevent lawsuits about debts.” Lightfoot ties it to Luke 12:58 and the lower Sanhedrin’s bench of three that judged money matters. Barnes reads it as still illustrating the sixth commandment — litigiousness being “a violation… of the law requiring us to love our neighbour” (1 Corinthians 6:7). Robertson glosses “agree” (be “kindly disposed”) and notes “compromise is better than prison where no principle is involved, but only personal interest,” and that the “officer” is the “under-rower” turned attendant. Clarke draws the evangelical use: “thou art a sinner; God hath a controversy with thee… now is the accepted time.”

The Latin fathers spiritualize the adversary. Augustine canvasses the candidates — devil, man, flesh, God, or his commandments — rejecting the devil (we “proclaimed war” against him at renunciation) and settling that the “adversary” best fits God’s word, with which we must “agree” while in this life. Hilary reads the adversary delivering us up when “the abiding of your wrath convicts you”; Pseudo-Chrysostom urges haste “to make friends with our enemies while we are yet in this life,” lest death find us at enmity. Chrysostom keeps it to “the judges of this world” and present prisons, as the things “before the eyes affect us most” (Romans 13:4).

The Reformed Calvin extends the counsel to both parties — even the one “unjustly treated” — tracing all injuries to self-love, “each too tenacious of his own rights.” Wesley reads “in the way” as “instantly, on the spot; before you part,” lest the adversary “commit his cause to God.” Clarke draws the prudential lesson that running “the whole course of a law-suit” is “vexatious… and in the end, though the loser may be ruined, yet the gainer has nothing.” Pseudo-Chrysostom presses the urgency spiritually: make peace “while we are yet in this life,” for if death finds us at enmity the Judge “will deliver us” guilty. The shared point: settle quarrels at once, before they harden into ruin.
26:
On Matthew 5:26 — “thou shalt by no means come out thence, till thou hast paid the uttermost farthing” — the witnesses agree the saying drives home the certainty and severity of the penalty, and divide over whether it implies a terminable or an endless punishment.

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the coin and the force. Lightfoot and Clarke trace the “farthing” (kodrantes) through the rabbinic reckoning of prutah, semissis, and assar, the prutah being “the very least piece among coins” (compare the two mites of Mark 12:42). Robertson notes the Latin loan-word and the “strong double negative” (ou mē) that paints “inevitable punishment for debt.” Ellicott explains the quadrans as the fourth of the Roman as, the smallest coin in circulation.

The chief debate concerns the duration. Ellicott observes that within “the frame-work of the similitude” the imprisonment would not be perpetual “if only the condemned could get together the money,” from which “Romanist divines have inferred” a purgatorial release; but he cautions against pressing a parable’s details into doctrine. The Wesleyan Wesley reads it the other way — “till thou hast paid the last farthing, that is, for ever, since thou canst never do this” — an endless penalty because the debt is unpayable. Robertson likewise stresses the inevitability rather than any release.

The grammatical-historical reading thus keeps the verse within its legal picture — a debtor held until full payment — while the application, on Wesley’s and Robertson’s view, warns of a reckoning from which there is no self-redemption. Either way the lesson stands: the quarrel unsettled in “the way” ends in a prison whose terms are not in the debtor’s power. Clarke turns it to evangelical use — the sinner, with God for his creditor and “but a step between thee and death,” is “invited to agree” while it is yet “the accepted time.” Lightfoot, fixing the coinage, notes the prutah was “the very least piece among coins,” so that “the uttermost farthing” names the last and smallest fraction of the debt, exacted in full — a vivid emblem of a justice that overlooks nothing.
27:
On Matthew 5:27 — “Ye have heard that it was said… Thou shalt not commit adultery” — the witnesses agree Christ takes up the seventh commandment as the scribes had narrowed it, in order to restore its inward reach.

The grammatical-historical commentators expose the gloss. Lightfoot shows the rabbis “interpreted it of the act of adultery only, and that with a married woman,” so the citation comes “deformed… with such a gloss as almost evacuated all the force of the command.” Barnes agrees the Pharisees made it “extend only to the external act,” counting “an evil thought and a wanton imagination” of little consequence; Christ assures them the commandment regards “the secrets of the heart.” On the introductory phrase, Clarke, Ellicott, and Robertson note that “by them of old time” is absent from the best manuscripts (struck out by Wetstein and Griesbach), the words here being simply the divine commandment as taught “in the narrowness of the letter.”

The Greek and Latin fathers stress the command’s breadth. John Chrysostom asks why this stands as the “second” commandment and not the Shema, answering that Christ could not yet begin there without “bringing in himself together with his Father.” Augustine widens the precept to all unchastity, charging the husband to “go before the wife in virtue” and the unmarried not to suppose fornication breaks it not, since “all such acts of lust pollute… God’s image.”

The Reformed Calvin states the governing principle for the section: the law “not only has authority over the life… to form the outward manners, but… requires pure and holy affections of the heart”; Christ blames not Moses but “the gross and false meaning” put on it, coming “as the faithful expounder.” The Puritan Henry adds that this law against uncleanness “fitly follows” that against sinful passions, both appetites needing the government of “reason and conscience,” and both, “if indulged, equally pernicious.” He notes the Pharisees made the command “extend no further than the act of adultery,” whereas Christ shows it “includes a prohibition of all other acts of uncleanness, and the desire of them.” Gregory, looking ahead to the remedy, counsels guarding the eyes “at the first,” since “the charm of beauty once admitted to the heart through the eye is hardly banished.”
28:
On Matthew 5:28 — “whosoever looketh on a woman to lust after her hath committed adultery with her already in his heart” — the witnesses agree the sin is located in the desiring look and the heart, before any act.

The grammatical-historical commentators weigh the verb and the limitation. Clarke renders “to lust after her” (epithumēsai) as “earnestly to covet,” a deliberate “impure desire,” not a passing thought; Ellicott sharpens it — “not the passing glance, not even the momentary impulse… but the continued gaze by which the impulse is deliberately cherished till it becomes a passion.” Robertson dwells on “in his heart” as “the inner man including the intellect, the affections, the will,” and quotes the rabbinic sayings that “the eye and the heart are the two brokers of sin” and “passions lodge only in him who sees” — hence “the peril of lewd pictures and plays.” Barnes cross-references Job 31:1 and Proverbs 6:25.

The Greek Chrysostom presses the danger of the habitual gaze — “if you permit yourself to gaze often on fair countenances you will assuredly be taken” — and applies the warning equally to the woman who “dresses and adorns herself for the purpose of attracting men’s eyes,” who “mixed the poison and offered the cup.” Gregory counsels averting the eyes “at the first,” since “the charm of beauty once admitted… is hardly banished.” Jerome distinguishes the involuntary first movement (propatheia) from the cherished passion that is sin.

The Reformed Calvin reads the verse by synecdoche — “not only the eyes, but even the concealed flames of the heart, render men guilty of adultery” — so that chastity is “both in body and in mind” (1 Corinthians 7:34). The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem cites the verse to prove Christ’s own sinlessness: he was “not condemned for concupiscence, for he himself says… whosoever shall look upon a woman to lust after her.” Gill marshals the severe Jewish prohibitions against gazing on a woman — that to do so even upon “a woman’s little finger” was reckoned grievous — yet shows the rabbis missed the heart of the matter, condemning the look while excusing the inward desire. The consensus is that Christ locates adultery “in the eye and heart before the outward act” (Robertson), so that the law’s purity searches the imagination itself.
29:
On Matthew 5:29 — “if thy right eye offend thee, pluck it out… for it is profitable” — the witnesses agree the command enjoins drastic self-denial against occasions of sin, and divide over how literally the members are meant.

The grammatical-historical commentators insist it is figurative. Robertson corrects “offend” to “cause to stumble” (skandalizei) — the bait-stick of a trap — and says the vivid pictures “are not to be taken literally, but powerfully plead for self-mastery”: “it is not mutilating of the body that Christ enjoins, but control of the body against sin.” Clarke: we must “shut our senses against dangerous objects… deprive ourselves of all that is most dear,” for men readily lose a limb to a surgeon to save life, yet will not give up “a look, a touch” that endangers the soul. Ellicott traces the difficulty of rendering “offend” through the English versions, and Barnes notes the right eye and hand are named as “of most use,” signifying that however strong the passion, it must be cut off (Mark 9:43).

The Latin fathers allegorize the members. Augustine takes the eye as a most-cherished friend or counsellor (“I love him as my own eye”) and the hand as action, so that whatever “hinders your true happiness” must be cut off. The Gloss reads the right eye as the contemplative life and the right hand as the active; Jerome, as the love of kin that may “hinder our view of the true light.”

The Reformed Calvin anticipates the complaint that this presses too hard on weak nature: “the justice of God ought to stand higher in our estimation than all that we reckon most precious” — better to part with your eyes than with God’s commandments, though Christ does not literally bid us maim ourselves. Wesley suggests a transposition, the eye-saying answering to vv.27–28 and the hand-saying to vv.21–22: “part with any thing, however dear… if you cannot avoid sin while you keep it.” Gill restricts the verse to the law of adultery, taking the eye as the instrument that often “ensnares the heart” and so must, figuratively, be cast away rather than allowed to lead into sin. Barnes draws the practical doctrine that “however strong the passion… or difficult to part with,” the offending occasion must go — the right eye and hand being named precisely because they are dearest and most useful, and therefore hardest to surrender.
30:
On Matthew 5:30 — “if thy right hand offend thee, cut it off” — the witnesses read the repetition as both emphatic and pointing to a distinct kind of temptation, the comment being brief and shared among the grammatical-historical and Reformed voices (with one rabbinic note).

The grammatical-historical Ellicott marks that the iteration is not mere repetition: “not the senses only, through which we receive impressions, but the gifts and energies which issue in action, may become temptations to evil,” and in that case “it were better to forfeit them.” The true remedy, he adds, is “so directing the will that eye and hand may each do its work in obedience to the law of righteousness.” Gill keeps it within the law of adultery: as adultery may be committed “in the heart, and by the eye, so with the hand” — “all unchaste touches, embraces” being condemned as the unchaste look was in v.28.

Lightfoot supplies the rabbinic background, citing the Babylonian tractate Niddah where Rabbi Tarphon pronounced that the wandering, unchaste hand “let it be cut off” — “it is better that his belly should be cleft in two, than that he should descend into the well of corruption” — a vivid parallel to the severity of Christ’s figure, though Christ’s point, as the grammatical-historical voices insist (see v.29), is self-mastery, not literal mutilation. The hand, the active member, stands for the deeds and gifts that, if they ensnare, must be surrendered rather than suffered to drag the whole man into ruin. Across the voices the doubled saying drives home that no faculty, however useful, may be kept at the cost of the soul. The same self-mastery urged for the eye (v.29) is now demanded of the hand, so that, as Ellicott puts it, “eye and hand may each do its work in obedience to the law of righteousness” rather than become a snare. The repetition is no idle doubling: it presses the same searching demand upon a second region of life, that what we receive and what we do alike be brought under the rule of purity, even at the cost of what we most prize.
31:
On Matthew 5:31 — “Whosoever shall put away his wife, let him give her a writing of divorcement” — the witnesses agree this is the Mosaic permission (Deuteronomy 24:1), granted for hardness of heart, which the scribes had abused.

The Latin fathers defend Moses against the charge of sanctioning divorce. Jerome: Moses allowed it “because of the hardness of the hearts of the husbands, not so much sanctioning discord, as checking bloodshed.” Pseudo-Chrysostom explains that Israel, “Hebrews in race, but Egyptians in manners,” would otherwise “kill or ill-treat” hated wives, so the bill was “the prevention of a worse evil.” Augustine, against Manichaeus, argues the very difficulty of obtaining the bill — drawn up only by the scribes skilled in the letters — would “often cool headlong rage,” showing Moses “no good friend to the practice.”

The grammatical-historical commentators set the contemporary controversy. Gill, Barnes, Clarke, and Ellicott note the dispute between the schools of Hillel and Shammai — the one permitting divorce “for any cause, however trivial” (Akiba: even finding a fairer woman; Josephus dismissing his wife “not being pleased with her manners”), the other only for adultery. Ellicott reads the introductory “but it was said” as stating an implied objection — men thinking to “avoid the sin of adultery by… divorcing one wife before marrying another.” Robertson glosses the “writing of divorcement” (apostasion) as a legal “bond of release,” a protection to the wife “against an angry whim of the husband.”

The Reformed Calvin (reserving fuller treatment for Matthew 19:9) explains that the Jews thought whatever the national law did not forbid was lawful; Moses had not commanded but only regulated divorce “for the sake of restraining lewdness,” the certificate being “a testimonial of freedom” for the wife. Gill notes the case of divorce was “not any law of God… but only a permission,” and that the very controversy over its grounds “was a new thing, just started in the time of Christ.” Pseudo-Chrysostom presses the Christian duty to “bear… the burdens of our wives and husbands” (Galatians 6:2) rather than seek release. The shared judgment is that the bill of divorce was a concession to human weakness, hedged about to restrain a greater evil, never a divine endorsement of the practice the scribes had made so easy.
32:
On Matthew 5:32 — “whosoever shall put away his wife, saving for the cause of fornication, causeth her to commit adultery” — the witnesses agree Christ restores the Mosaic permission to its true, narrow bounds, and discuss the exception and its consequence.

The grammatical-historical commentators define the limits. Lightfoot lays out three points: Christ “does not abrogate Moses’ permission… but tolerates it,” keeping it “within the Mosaic bounds” (the case of adultery) and condemning the canon that allowed divorce “for any cause”; that even there divorce is “permitted, not commanded”; and that the unjustly divorced wife is driven toward a second, adulterous marriage. Robertson defends the exception clause (parektos logou porneias, “except for a matter of unchastity”) against McNeile’s claim that the early Church inserted it because Mark and Luke omit it — “one fails to see the force of this charge.” Ellicott takes “fornication” in its “most generic” sense, perhaps including ante-nuptial sin, and notes that “causeth her to commit adultery” implies the divorce was “legally a divorce à vinculo,” leaving both free to remarry. Clarke insists a Christian should rather “beg of God the grace to bear patiently… the imperfections of his wife.”

The Reformed Calvin and Gill agree Christ “does not infringe the original… permission of divorce; only frees it from the false interpretations” of the Pharisees, restoring its ancient sense; the man who “unjustly abandons the wife whom God had given him” is condemned for “having prostituted his wife to others.” The Alexandrian Origen, as an outlier, records a Jewish objector who would equate Moses’s “unseemly thing” with fornication, and answers that, since the adulteress was to be stoned, the exception cannot simply reinstate the old license. Wesley sums the consequence: he “causeth her to commit adultery — if she marry again.” Lightfoot adds that even in the case of adultery Christ “does not impose a compulsion to divorce, but indulgeth a license to do it,” against the Jewish practice that sometimes compelled it. The settled sense across the witnesses is that Christ neither abolishes Moses’s provision nor widens it, but pares away the Pharisaic license “for every cause,” binding husband and wife to a permanence broken only by the one sin that has already broken the marriage bond.
33:
On Matthew 5:33 — “Thou shalt not forswear thyself, but shalt perform unto the Lord thine oaths” — the witnesses agree Christ turns from duty to neighbour to reverence toward God, taking up the third commandment as the scribes had narrowed it.

The grammatical-historical commentators expose the gloss. Lightfoot shows the “Fathers of the Traditions reduced the whole sin of swearing” to perjury, “little caring for a rash oath,” so long as what was sworn was true. Barnes notes the law (Leviticus 19:12; Deuteronomy 23:23) forbade swearing falsely and required oaths to be performed “literally, really, and religiously.” Ellicott explains that the rabbis “did not see that the holy name might be profaned in other ways,” and so “expressly or tacitly allowed many forms of oath in which it was not named.”

The Latin fathers press toward avoidance of oaths altogether. Augustine: since perjury is grievous, “he must be further removed from it who uses no oath,” God preferring “that we should not swear and keep close to the truth”; yet he notes the Apostle calls God to witness (Galatians 1:20; Romans 1:9). Jerome observes Christ here forbids swearing “by the heaven, the earth… a man’s head,” the creatures to which the Jews paid “the honour belonging to God.” Pseudo-Chrysostom: “no man can swear often, but he must sometimes forswear himself.”

The Reformed Calvin states the principle: this is “not a correction of the law, but a true interpretation,” for the law condemned not only perjury but “lightness in swearing, which lessens the reverence for his name” (Exodus 20:7); the Jews wrongly “imagined that the guilt lay entirely in acts of perjury.” The Puritan Henry adds that the third commandment, which God “will not hold him guiltless” who breaks, forbids taking God’s name “unto vanity, or a lie.” Wesley links it back to seeing God in all things, a vision the scribes’ false doctrine had lost. Chrysostom observes that Christ “exalts the elements… not from their own nature, but from the respect which they have to God,” lest any occasion of idolatry be opened. Lightfoot details the rabbinic ranks of oaths — promissory, vain, and the rest — showing how the “Fathers of the Traditions” troubled themselves only that what was sworn be true, “little caring for a rash oath.” The consensus is that the third commandment guards the whole reverence due to God’s name, which careless and creature-oaths profane.
34:
On Matthew 5:34 — “But I say unto you, Swear not at all… neither by heaven; for it is God’s throne” — the witnesses divide over whether Christ forbids all oaths or only rash and creature-oaths.

The grammatical-historical and Reformed voices hold the latter. Robertson: “certainly Jesus does not prohibit oaths in a court of justice, for he himself answered Caiaphas on oath” (Matthew 26:63–64), and Paul made solemn appeals to God (1 Corinthians 15:31); “Jesus prohibits all forms of profanity.” Calvin warns that “many have been led by the phrase to adopt the false notion that every kind of swearing is condemned”; the immediate context shows the forbidden kinds — the Jews’ “circuitous or indirect ways of swearing” by creatures — are added “by way of exposition.” Gill: not lawful oaths in weighty matters “in the name of God” but “rash swearing, about trivial matters, and by the creatures.” Lightfoot sets Christ’s rule “in the middle way, between the Jew, who allowed some place for an arbitrary oath, and the Anabaptist, who allows none for a necessary one.”

Against this, Ellicott reports the view of “whole Christian communities, as the Society of Friends,” who, with James 5:12, see “a formal prohibition of all oaths,” and urges their scruples be treated “with great tenderness,” though he judges the conclusion mistaken.

On the creature-oaths, Barnes and Clarke explain that to swear “by heaven” is really to swear by God enthroned there (Matthew 23:22): either oaths by inanimate things are “unmeaning and wicked,” or, if real, they invoke the living God whose property they are. The forbidden oath, on the dominant reading, is the profane and the evasive, not the reverent and necessary. Clarke laments how “swearing in civil matters is become so frequent that the dread and obligation of an oath are utterly lost,” men even kissing the thumb instead of the book to dodge the guilt of perjury — “a shocking imposition on their own souls.” Calvin warns that “some good men have been driven to this extreme rigor” of condemning all oaths “by observing the unbridled licentiousness of swearing”; but the cure is not to forbid the lawful oath, only the creature-oaths Christ proceeds to specify.
35:
On Matthew 5:35 — “nor by the earth, for it is his footstool; neither by Jerusalem, for it is the city of the great King” — the comment is brief and chiefly grammatical-historical, continuing the exposure of the Jews’ oaths by creatures.

Gill documents from the rabbinic instances that “the Jews were wont to swear by the earth,” and that Christ condemns it “because the earth is God’s footstool” — that on which he treads and where “he manifests forth his glory,” a chief part of “the work of his hands.” The principle, common to this verse and its neighbours, is that every such oath, however it seems to skirt the divine name, in fact reaches back to God: to swear by the earth is to swear by him whose footstool it is, and to swear by Jerusalem is to swear by him whose city it is. Barnes marks the title “the city of the great King,” pointing to the holy city of God (compare Psalm 48:2; Revelation 21:2), so that an oath by Jerusalem is no escape from invoking its King.

Thus the verse drives home what the preceding one began (v.34): the elaborate Jewish system of swearing “by” this creature or that, devised to avoid pronouncing God’s name and so to leave a margin for evasion, is hollow. Heaven, earth, and Jerusalem are all so bound to God — his throne, his footstool, his city — that to swear by them is, whether the swearer admits it or not, to swear by God himself, and so to incur the full guilt of a careless or false oath. As Jerome observes, the man who swears by a thing “shows either reverence or love for that by which he swears,” so that the Jews, swearing by heaven, earth, the temple, and Jerusalem, “paid to the creature the honour and worship belonging to God” — the very fault the prophets had charged on them. Thus the elaborate casuistry of binding and non-binding oaths (compare Matthew 23:16–22) collapses: there is no creature so detached from its Maker that an oath by it escapes him. The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note attached here is misplaced, commenting on salt and the Beatitudes rather than on oaths.
36:
