On Matthew 5:36 — “Neither shalt thou swear by thy head, because thou canst not make one hair white or black” — the witnesses, almost all grammatical-historical, note this as the extreme instance of a creature-oath that yet implies God.

Gill records that swearing “by thy head” was “a common form of swearing among the Jews,” and Barnes adds it was used among the Gentiles also: to swear by the head “was the same as to swear by the life… I will forfeit my life if what I say is not true.” Since “God is the Author of the life, to swear by that is the same as to swear by him.” Ellicott reads it as “an extreme instance of a common oath in which men found no reference to God,” yet “nothing but an implied reference to him fits it to be an oath at all”: he made us, “and the hairs of our head… are subject in all their changes to his laws, and not to our volition.” Lightfoot glosses “make one hair white or black” as “thou canst not put on gray hairs, or lay them aside” — a man cannot govern even so small a thing as the colour of his hair, far less make good an oath sworn on his own life.

Clarke draws the fourfold reason the head is not to be sworn by: “God commands thee not”; “thou hast nothing which is thy own, and thou shouldst not pledge another’s property”; “it never did, and never can, answer any good purpose”; and “being a breach of the law of God, it is the way to everlasting misery.” The cumulative point of vv.34–36 is that there is no neutral creature to swear by: heaven, earth, the holy city, and a man’s own life all belong to God, so every such oath is, at bottom, an appeal to him, and the multiplication of them only multiplies the peril of profaning his name. Gill documents that swearing “by the head” was, like the rest, “a common form” in the Jewish schools, which Christ here cuts off at the root; and Clarke presses that such oaths “never did, and never can, answer any good purpose,” being a breach of God’s law and so “the way to everlasting misery” — a weighty reckoning for what men treated as a trifle.
37:
On Matthew 5:37 — “let your communication be, Yea, yea; Nay, nay: for whatsoever is more than these cometh of evil” — the witnesses agree the remedy for swearing is plain truthfulness, and divide only on whether “evil” is abstract or personal.

The grammatical-historical commentators read it of sincere speech. Gill takes the doubled “yea” and “nay” as “the stronger asseveration… when it is necessary,” but “let no oaths be joined unto them” — “a righteous man’s yea is yea, and his no is no.” Clarke: “a positive affirmation, or negation… do not equivocate; mean what you assert,” quoting Homer that the man “whose words agree not with his private thoughts” is hateful “as the gates of hell.” Ellicott notes the precept is reproduced in James 5:12 and was “probably proverbial” — “in all common speech a man’s words should be as good as his oath.” Barnes draws the lessons that “profane swearing is always the evidence of a depraved heart” and that no man “is believed any sooner… because he swears.” Rabanus adds that “yea, yea; nay, nay” are doubled so that “what you affirm with the mouth you should prove in deed.”

On “cometh of evil,” Ellicott weighs whether the Greek is neuter (“evil in the abstract”) or masculine (“the evil one,” as in the Lord’s Prayer), inclining “with some hesitation” to the abstract.

The Reformed Calvin names the source of the disease: men swear much because of “the numerous impositions… so that hardly any thing is believed”; the cure is that “men act towards each other sincerely and honestly,” for then “simplicity of speech will have quite as much weight as an oath.” Hilary: those who live “in the simplicity of the faith have not need to swear,” with them “what is is, what is not is not.” Gill notes that “a righteous man’s yea is yea, and his no is no; his word is sufficient,” so that the doubling is for “the stronger asseveration… when it is necessary,” never an oath. The witnesses thus locate the cure of swearing not in finer rules about oaths but in a recovered truthfulness, where plain speech needs no reinforcement because the speaker is believed.
38:
On Matthew 5:38 — “An eye for an eye, and a tooth for a tooth” — the witnesses agree this was a judicial rule limiting revenge, which the scribes had perverted into a license for private vengeance.

The Latin fathers explain the law’s purpose. Augustine: it “was enacted to repress the flames of mutual hate,” since the avenger is never “content to return just so much harm as he had received”; the lex talionis “puts bounds… not to encourage but to check rage.” Jerome: “the Law corrects faults, the Gospel removes their occasions.” John Chrysostom defends the old law against those who fault it, urging “the virtue of opportunities” suited to its first hearers.

The grammatical-historical commentators make the same distinction. Barnes: as “a rule to regulate the decisions of judges… it is not unjust,” and Christ “finds no fault with the rule as applied to magistrates,” but the Jews had “extended it to private conduct” as a warrant for revenge. Robertson: “like divorce this is a restriction upon unrestrained vengeance… it limited revenge by fixing an exact compensation” (Exodus 21:24; Leviticus 24:20). Ellicott calls it “a limit in both directions” — not less, lest crime be connived at; not more, lest a fresh wrong begin. Lightfoot shows the rabbis had resolved the law “not into a just retaliation, but into a pecuniary compensation.”

The Reformed Calvin agrees: God enjoined that magistrates “punish… by making them endure as much as they had inflicted,” but men “seized on this as a pretext for taking private revenge,” whereas “it is the duty of every man to bear patiently the injuries which he receives.” The Puritan Henry: the law of retaliation is here “expounded, and in a manner repealed” for private life, being appointed “to be done by the magistrate… the minister of God, an avenger.” Pseudo-Chrysostom adds the practical wisdom of non-resistance: a return blow has never “restrained him from striking you again” but “rather roused him,” for “anger is not checked by meeting anger, but is only more irritated.” Lightfoot confirms from the rabbis that the law was in practice resolved “into a pecuniary compensation,” the maimed party paid according to a fixed valuation — so that even the Jews did not take “eye for eye” with literal severity, though they kept its vengeful spirit.
39:
On Matthew 5:39 — “resist not evil: but whosoever shall smite thee on thy right cheek, turn to him the other also” — the witnesses agree the command forbids private retaliation, and discuss both its precise object and how far it is literal.

The grammatical-historical commentators weigh “evil.” Clarke and Ellicott take it as masculine — “the evil person” or “human evil-doer” (as in 1 Corinthians 5:13), not “the evil one” whom we must resist (James 4:7) — while Robertson notes the dative serves “either view” (the evil man or the injury). All insist the words are “not to be pressed too literally”: Robertson recalls that Jesus protested when smitten (John 18:22), “paradoxes startle and make us think”; Clarke paraphrases “do not repel one outrage by another,” for he that does “makes himself precisely what the other is.” Lightfoot sets against it the Jewish scale of fines for a blow on the cheek, which Christ’s “turn the other” utterly transcends.

The fathers read it morally. Pseudo-Chrysostom: “anger is not checked by meeting anger, but is only more irritated”; if we resist not, “they that are good remain good.” Jerome offers a mystical sense — the heretic who smites our “right cheek” in disputation is to be met “with another testimony from Scripture.”

The Reformed Calvin distinguishes “two ways of resisting”: warding off injury “by inoffensive conduct” (which Christ does not forbid) and “retaliation” (which he does), citing Paul’s “overcome evil with good” (Romans 12:21). Wesley notes “resist” signifies “standing in battle array, striving for victory”: return not evil for evil “rather than revenge thyself.” Gill guards the lawful right of self-defence and of redress before “the civil magistrate,” forbidding only “private revenge” — the “evil” here being not sin or Satan, who must be resisted, but “an injurious man.” Clarke renders the maxim “do not repel one outrage by another,” warning that he who does “makes himself precisely what the other is, a wicked person,” and applies the precept chiefly to “those who are persecuted for righteousness’ sake,” who must “leave the judgment of their cause to him for whose sake they suffer.”
40:
On Matthew 5:40 — “if any man will sue thee at the law, and take away thy coat, let him have thy cloke also” — the witnesses agree this turns the law of non-retaliation to petty litigation, counselling forbearance over insistence on rights.

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the garments and the scene. Robertson: the “coat” (chitōn) is “a sort of shirt or undergarment and would be demanded at law,” the “cloke” (himation) the more valuable outer garment a robber would seize first — so if one loses the undergarment at law, “the outer one goes also.” Clarke notes the Lord’s “utmost disapprobation of such litigations as tended to destroy brotherly kindness,” preferring “the loss of all their property” to contention at “so great a risk.” Ellicott renders it more strongly — the man “is bent on going to law” — and aligns it with Paul’s “why do ye not rather suffer yourselves to be defrauded?” (1 Corinthians 6:7). Lightfoot identifies the coat (talith) as the outer garment bearing the fringes the Jews gloried in, sharpening the disgrace of its loss.

The Reformed Calvin guards against a wooden reading: “none but a fool will stand upon the words,” as if we must yield whatever opponents demand before any trial, “for such compliance would more strongly inflame the minds of wicked men to robbery.” The point is patient submission rather than vengeful litigation over “purely personal” wrongs. Wesley sums the whole passage: “rather than return evil for evil, when the wrong is purely personal, submit to one bodily wrong after another, give up one part of your goods after another” — and that “the words are not literally to be understood appears from the behaviour of our Lord himself” (John 18:23). Clarke adds that Christ everywhere shows “the utmost disapprobation of such litigations as tended to destroy brotherly kindness,” counting it gain “when we lose only our money… and risk not the loss of our souls.” Lightfoot’s detail that the seized garment (the talith) bore the very fringes the Jews gloried in heightens the demand: a disciple must yield even what carries his religious pride rather than feed a quarrel.
41:
On Matthew 5:41 — “whosoever shall compel thee to go a mile, go with him twain” — the witnesses agree the picture is of forced public service, and the lesson is willing endurance beyond what is exacted.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the word “compel” (angareuō). Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott, and Robertson trace it to the Persian postal system, where royal couriers (angaroi) were “permitted to compel any person, or to press any horse… for the quick transmission of the king’s commandments” (Herodotus viii. 98). Ellicott notes the Roman empire adopted the same practice — soldiers “billeted on Jewish householders,” men “impressed for service” — and that “mile” is the Latin mille passuum, a mark of Rome’s presence. Robertson recalls the very word used of Simon of Cyrene, who was “compelled to carry the cross of Christ” (Matthew 27:32). Gill cites the rabbinic use of the term for “the taking of anything for the service of the king.”

Lightfoot fits the saying into the Jewish scale of legal redress for bodily wrong — the five “mulcts” for maiming, pain, cure, reproach, and enforced idleness — against which Christ sets a wholly different temper: not the exacting of compensation but the doubling of the burden willingly borne. Barnes draws the application: “rather than resist a public authority… submit to the inconvenience, and show the spirit that will not contend.” The grammatical-historical consensus is that the verse, like its neighbours, paints in vivid, concrete terms the disciple’s freedom from the spirit of grudging resistance — meeting even an unjust impressment with generosity rather than complaint. Clarke adds the vivid detail that a Persian courier, when his horse tired, “dismounts the first man he meets, and takes his horse,” there being “no pardon for a traveller that disputes” — so the impressment Christ names was a hated, arbitrary power, met not with resentment but with willing excess. Gill notes the term covers “any servile work” pressed for the king’s service. The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note attached here is misplaced, dwelling on the divine authority of “I say unto you” rather than on the verse itself.
42:
On Matthew 5:42 — “Give to him that asketh thee, and from him that would borrow of thee turn not thou away” — the witnesses agree the command teaches a generous, giving temper, and warn that its letter is not an absolute rule.

The grammatical-historical commentators read it as a guarded paradox. Robertson calls it “one of the clearest instances of the necessity of accepting the spirit and not the letter,” since “indiscriminate almsgiving does little but injury to society” — though the words “embrace far more than almsgiving.” Barnes lays down that it is “better to give sometimes to an undeserving person than to turn away one really necessitous,” yet the rule must be consistent with duty to one’s family (1 Timothy 5:8) and not encourage idleness (2 Thessalonians 3:10). Ellicott calls it “a rule which in its letter is binding upon no man,” teaching rather “the loving and the giving temper” that treats every request as “a call to thoughtful inquiry how best to help.” Clarke urges that “both prudence and charity must be consulted,” remembering that the God who “makes use of the beggar’s hand” is the same from whom we beg our daily bread.

The Reformed Calvin appeals to the parallel in Luke 6:30: Christ would make his disciples “generous, but not prodigals,” for “it would be a foolish prodigality to scatter at random what the Lord has given us”; liberality is not discharged by aiding “a few persons” but governed by the Spirit’s rule. Gill stresses that giving should be “freely, readily, cheerfully, according to your abilities, and as the necessity of the object requires,” with special regard “to the household of faith.” Wesley sums it: give and lend “so far… as is consistent with thy engagements to thy creditors, thy family, and the household of faith,” for “God never contradicts himself.” Barnes adds the test of prudence: it is “seldom, perhaps never, good to give to a man that is able to work” (2 Thessalonians 3:10) — “if such a man is indeed hungry, feed him; if he wants anything farther, give him employment.” The dominant reading, then, is that the verse commands a heart always open and ready to give, while leaving the manner and measure to a charity governed by wisdom and by our other God-given duties.
43:
On Matthew 5:43 — “Thou shalt love thy neighbour, and hate thine enemy” — the witnesses agree the first clause is the Law (Leviticus 19:18) and the second a scribal addition, and that the Jews had wrongly narrowed “neighbour.”

The grammatical-historical commentators and the Reformed are emphatic that “hate thine enemy” is “nowhere written” in the Law. Robertson: “a rabbinical inference which Jesus repudiates bluntly… the Talmud says nothing of love to enemies.” The Gloss notes it was added by the scribes from Israel’s commission against the Canaanites. Clarke and Lightfoot show that the Jews took “neighbour” to mean fellow-Jew or friend (plēsion as “friend,” Luke 10:36), Lightfoot citing their “poisonous canons” of eternal hatred toward Gentiles. Ellicott grants the law practically bade Israel hate Amalek, but faults the scribes for stereotyping a transitory command and extending it “in a wrong direction” into private enmity. Henry: from “love thy neighbour” they inferred “what God never designed.”

The Latin and Greek fathers open the true breadth. Augustine, by the Good Samaritan, shows “our neighbour is every one who may… stand in need of our offices of mercy.” Pseudo-Chrysostom distinguishes flesh and spirit: “the flesh hates our enemy, but our soul loves him.” Jerome insists Christ “enjoins not impossibilities but perfection,” instancing David, Stephen, Paul, and Christ’s own “Father, forgive them” (Luke 23:34). Chrysostom traces the nine ascending steps to “the very pinnacle of virtue,” ending in love and prayer for the enemy, the reward being “to be made like unto God.”

The Reformed Calvin marvels the scribes could so limit a word in which “God… includes the whole human race”: “whenever I see a man, I must… behold myself as in a mirror,” for he is my own flesh (Genesis 29:14). Gregory adds a fine test of whether we truly love an enemy: love is kept “when we are not sorrowful at his success, or rejoice in his fall” — though he allows that a fall may gladden us without malice when by it “some deserving man is raised up.” Wesley, with Henry, fixes the verdict: “God spoke the former part; the scribes added the latter” — so the hatred of enemies is no part of the Law but a human corruption of it.
44:
On Matthew 5:44 — “Love your enemies… pray for them which… persecute you” — the witnesses hail this as the height of the law of love, and distinguish the kind of love that is commanded.

The grammatical-historical commentators define the love. Barnes separates “the love of complacency,” by which we approve another’s conduct, from “the love of benevolence,” by which “we wish well to the person” though we cannot approve his conduct — and it is the latter we owe our enemies: “though we may hate his conduct… we may still wish well to the man.” Clarke calls it “the most sublime piece of morality ever given to man,” reasonable because hatred makes the hater “necessarily miserable,” though obeyable only by “him who has the mind of Christ.” Ellicott notes that the clause “do good to them that hate you” is omitted in many ancient manuscripts (likely assimilated from Luke 6:28), and stresses prayer as “the highest utterance of that love.”

The Reformed Calvin makes this the summary of all the foregoing: “he who shall bring his mind to love those who hate him will naturally refrain from all revenge… and be much more prone to assist the wretched” (Matthew 22:39); but no one obeys it “till he shall have… denied himself.” Gill clarifies that enemies are to be loved “with a natural love, as men,” their actions still hated, citing the apostolic “if thine enemy hunger, feed him” (Romans 12:20). Wesley: “speak all the good you can to and of them who speak all evil… repay love in thought, word, and deed” (Luke 6:27). The shared conviction is that the gospel commands not a feeling we cannot summon but a settled good-will, crowned by prayer, toward those who wrong us. Clarke insists the precept, far from absurd, is “one of the most reasonable” in the universe, since “he is necessarily miserable who hates another” — yet obeyable only by him who, lacking the mind of Christ, will “seek it” from God. Wesley spells out the love commanded: “repay love in thought, word, and deed, to those who hate you, and show it both in word and deed” (Luke 6:35).
45:
On Matthew 5:45 — “that ye may be the children of your Father… who maketh his sun to rise on the evil and on the good” — the witnesses agree the verse grounds love of enemies in likeness to God’s impartial bounty.

The grammatical-historical and Reformed voices guard against a doctrine of earning sonship. Gill: men become God’s children “not by the works they do… but by adopting grace”; the meaning is that they may “appear, and be known to be the children of God” by resembling him — “as the tree is known by its fruit.” Barnes and Clarke note several manuscripts read “that ye may be like” your Father, “certainly our Lord’s meaning”: as a child shares his father’s nature, so the holy man, made “partaker of the divine nature,” imitates God “in doing good to our enemies.” Ellicott renders “that ye may become”: we cannot grow like God “in power or wisdom” — that attempt caused the Fall — “but we cannot err in striving to be like him in his love” (Ephesians 5:1), the beneficence “seen in Nature” of sun and rain.

The Latin Pseudo-Chrysostom observes that in bestowing his gifts God “does not separate the sinners from the righteous,” sending sun and rain on both; Remigius notes “as” here expresses “likeness only,” not equality. The Reformed Calvin presses the verse as “an express command” — “whoever shall wish to be accounted a Christian, let him” love his enemies — and laments that the world lay “in such thick darkness” as not to see that he who neglects it “is struck out of the number of the children of God”; yet he cautions that imitation of God does not extend to all God does, since God also “punishes the wicked.” Wesley: such love shows we “continue and appear” God’s children before men and angels, receiving “such blessings as they will receive at his hands.” Pseudo-Chrysostom presses the impartial bounty — God gives his good gifts “not for men’s sake, but for the saints’ sake,” not separating sinners from the righteous in sun and rain — so that to love only friends is to fall below the pattern of the Father whose kindness reaches even his enemies. The verse thus makes love of enemies not an optional height but the very mark by which the children of God are known.
46:
On Matthew 5:46 — “if ye love them which love you, what reward have ye? do not even the publicans the same?” — the witnesses agree Christ shames a merely reciprocal love by comparing it to the most despised of men.

The grammatical-historical commentators draw out the point. Gill clarifies Christ does not forbid loving those who love us, but forbids making “these the only objects of your love.” Barnes explains “reward” as desert of praise: to love only those who love us is “selfish… not disinterested… love for the benefit,” deserving “no commendation,” since “the very publicans would do the same.” On the publicans, Barnes, Clarke, Lightfoot, and Wesley describe the tax-gatherers as “objects of great detestation” among the Jews, “reckoned… the very scum of the earth,” abhorred for “intolerable rapacity and avarice” and as agents of a foreign yoke; Lightfoot cites the rabbinic ruling that their money was presumed got “by rapine.”

Ellicott observes our Lord “puts himself… on the level of those to whom he speaks”: they despised the publicans “as below them, almost as a Pariah caste,” yet were they really “above the publicans, if they confined their love to a reciprocity of good offices?” The Reformed Calvin notes the office of tax-collector is “not condemned in itself,” princes having the right to levy taxes; the publicans are so named because such men were “usually covetous and rapacious… the agents of a wicked tyranny.” His meaning, Calvin adds, is that “those who are nearly devoid of humanity have some appearance of discharging mutual duties” toward those who serve their interest — a standard the disciple must far exceed. Clarke sharpens the principle: “he who loves only his friends does nothing for God’s sake… he who loves for the sake of pleasure or interest pays himself,” God having “no enemy which he hates but sin; we should have no other.” The reciprocal affection of the natural man, the witnesses agree, however amiable, earns no reward, because it costs nothing and rises no higher than self-love. Lightfoot rehearses how “odious the publicans were to the Jewish nation,” their money presumed “gotten by rapine” and not to be mingled with an honest man’s; yet these very outcasts “love them which love them.” Gill clarifies that Christ does not forbid loving those who love us, only the making of “these the only objects of your love” — the disciple’s love must overflow the narrow channel of mere requital.
47:
On Matthew 5:47 — “if ye salute your brethren only, what do ye more than others? do not even the heathen so?” — the witnesses agree Christ presses the same lesson from the social custom of greeting, and the comment is wholly grammatical-historical (with one Wesleyan application).

Several commentators fix the cultural force. Ellicott notes “the prominence of salutation in the social life of the East”: to utter the formal “Peace be with you” and follow it with “manifold compliments” was “to recognise those whom men saluted as friends and brothers” — and he adopts the better reading “heathen” (rather than “publicans”), so that the question is whether Christ’s followers are to be “content with copying heathen customs.” Barnes explains that to “salute” is to show “the customary tokens of civility”; Christians should “treat their enemies as well as wicked men did their friends,” both because it is right and to show “that religion is not selfish.” Clarke notes the variant “friends” for “brethren” and gathers from Harmer the weight given to salutations in Asiatic countries. Gill takes the salutation as “by words, asking of each other’s welfare, and wishing prosperity.”

The Wesleyan Wesley turns it against sectarianism: our Lord “probably glances at those prejudices which different sects had against each other,” not wishing his followers to “imbibe that narrow spirit” — a wish, he laments, too little attended to “among the unhappy divisions… into which his Church has been crumbled,” that we might at least “cordially embrace our brethren in Christ, of whatever party.” The shared point is that a love confined to one’s own circle rises no higher than nature’s, and so is no mark of grace. Barnes draws the two reasons Christians should greet even their enemies: “because it is right… the only really amiable spirit,” and to “show that religion is not selfish, and is superior to all other principles of action.” Gill takes the salutation as the customary words “asking of each other’s welfare, and wishing prosperity” — a courtesy the disciple must extend beyond the bounds of party and even to the hostile, if his love is to outshine the heathen’s.
48:
On Matthew 5:48 — “Be ye therefore perfect, even as your Father which is in heaven is perfect” — the witnesses agree the “perfection” is likeness to God, especially in love, and divide on its exact scope.

The grammatical-historical commentators define the word. Robertson: teleios comes from telos, “end, goal” — “the goal set before us, the absolute standard of our Heavenly Father,” though also used of “relative perfection as of adults compared with children.” Barnes traces it from a finished machine “complete in its parts,” applied to Job (Job 1:1) not as sinless but as “proportionate… consistent and regular” in piety. Ellicott renders the “ideal future that implies an imperative”: the perfection is “the attainment of the end… of our being,” which in us “implies growth” but in God “exists eternally” — we partake of it “when we love as he loves.” Clarke makes the words “both a command and a promise,” God being “the sole giver and only pattern” of the perfection he enjoins.

The Reformed Calvin and Gill restrain the perfection to the subject in hand — love — and read “as” of resemblance, not equality: Luke’s parallel has “be ye merciful” (Luke 6:36); “however distant we are from the perfection of God, we are said to be perfect when we aim at the same object which he presents to us in himself.” Gill: the “as” is “not a note of equality, but of likeness,” bidding us imitate the Father in extending love to the same objects as his goodness does. Wesley hears the verse seal “all his commandments with a promise” — that God “will put those laws in our minds, and write them in our hearts” — against the unbelief that cries “this is impossible.” Clarke likewise reads it as “both a command and a promise,” God being “the grand law-giver, sole giver, and only pattern of the perfection he recommends to his children” — the Father whose name is Mercy and whose nature is love. The chapter thus closes by gathering all the foregoing precepts into a single goal: to be made like the Father, supremely in that love which reaches even enemies. (A stray Cyril of Jerusalem fragment on the angels’ vision of God is attached here, unrelated to the verse.)
